speaker: interviewer
text: Number three. We're beginning now with life in the Krakow Ghetto. Halina, could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You call this life?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could call it life, could you describe how you might have characterized it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very unreal. And at the beginning, I think that we all felt-- at least I felt-- it is something like, let's say, temporarily, vacation, or whatever. My mother had a wonderful saying. She always said, 'Nothing takes such a long time as temporally, or in the meantime.' And in the meantime, one has to live. So we really-- the Germans said that they are making ghetto in order to save us from the anti-Semitic Poles. What was, of course, ridiculous. And then-- so not knowing Yiddish, and not knowing Hebrew, when we were working with my mother and some other people too who were also not-- well, like-- like we-- on the streets, we could not even read the signs on the stores, because they were all supposed to be in Hebrew or in Yiddish, and not in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, during the first two or three months, it was not so bad. It could be worse, we thought. There-- but later on, when the walls were fixed, and it was not allowed to go out without special permission-- and who got special permission? Almost no one. So therefore, it was very difficult, and nobody knew how to organize our life. And then this is when people, themselves, say, someone has to take care of the orphanage. Someone had to take care of the hospital. At this time, my brother was already home, and he was working in the Jewish hospital. Having already been trained?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was-- as-- well, how do you call? Like a trained nurse or assistant to a, by the way, very famous surgeon, Dr. Nussenfeld. Well-- so he was working there. And my father, who didn't wanted-- well, to be connected with this so-called, Jewish-- how to say, Jewish elders, [GERMAN]-- well, you know-- I don't know what is that English word for it. Well, there was-- there was the head of the community. So-- and there was already Jewish police, Ordenienst, called OD. Well, but they were the people who were preparing least for deportation. And all these things, my father didn't wanted this. And, therefore, he choose to be head of this sanitation department. And at least, he thought he will be able to be useful socially by cleaning-- his people were cleaning the ghetto, and the streets, and so forth. And Mother was working with the orphanage. And I attended still the clandestine classes. And also, since there was no school, I took care of some two or three small children whose parents were working outside of the ghetto, so that the kids will have someone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was making drawings for them. And I rewrote my own children's book. And so I was like a nanny for them. But not too long, because then when there was the first big action-- action it was called-- then all the children left.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was something else also. That this-- they-- the words the Germans used, like Aussiedlung, translocation, or so, they sound quite innocent. Nobody knew that this is translocation out of this world. So then, suddenly, people who were in the suburbs of Krakow were coming to the Krakow Ghetto. And all of a sudden, the Germans directed them to come to the Krakow ghetto. And there, there were so many of us that really there was-- even when we came to get off from our apartment, the apartment first in ghetto, it was one room and one kitchen. And then they did not know what to do with so many people. Well, the head of the Jewish elders, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they found out that it will be the best when they will count the windows, and there will be three people on one window. Since we had three windows-- one in the kitchen and two in room-- it means that we had to live with nine people together in that one. But within that was you, your mom, your dad, and your brother, among the nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then others. And then so we exchanged, yes, and moved into one room-- just one room. And there, we were with our engineer uncle, Ignaci, and one of his boys. His other boy and wife were staying with her sisters in another apartment. And so we were there. And in the meantime, everybody had to work. And I could not work anymore with the children when the children were not there. So I was working in an electrical store, selling electric bulbs. But it was not such a big place to be, because there were maybe 10 people coming during the day. People did not have money, and it was some kind of mixture that people who were like intelligentsia, who were always straightforward and, well, were making money on their mind, on their brain, on their abilities and profession. They did not have this. And that people who were very energetic and very ruthless, suddenly, they become the nouveau-riche, and they had money. And they could do whatever they want to. And then, it become these things with cleaning the streets, and cleaning from snow, and this and that, all kind of this. And then suddenly, it was first in June. It was the great deportation, when for about a week, the Germans rounded the ghetto and were taking--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is now June of--

speaker: interviewee
text: June of '41 or '42. Let me check with my diary, because we just talk and talk. Mm-hmm. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I think 1941. So it was June. It was-- first, it was in June, and another one was in October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the same year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the same year. And well, we just simply couldn't understand. I, at least, I couldn't understand, and I couldn't comprehend how it comes that suddenly people are here, and then they are taken and they disappear. And nobody knows where they are going. And it was said that they are going to the east, to Russia to work, or somewhere. But you never heard about these people. And then when there were coming such strange things, that they were-- they were-- they were being taken to gas. There were massacres and this. First of all, it's also something. Every ghetto was so isolated that we absolutely had no idea what was going on in the world, so what was going on in other towns, even in another-- in other villages. We were like uniquely for ourselves. There was no communication, whatsoever. And after this June terrible, well, very bad deportation-- or Aussiedlung, as the German call it-- then there was-- it was said that we all have to work. Everybody has to work, and we went to forced jobs. Some people didn't wanted to go outside of the ghetto, because they were the very menial jobs. Well-- but it was not even that they were menial, and that people were being beaten there, and this and that. Well, we had to go and work. And this was in that-- barracks-- in SS barracks, Gestapo, well, soldiers, Luftwaffe, and so forth, all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, I had to go, too. My parents first didn't wanted to, because they knew, for heaven's sake, what we are going to do there-- you will scrub the floors, and this and that. So-- but the one thing what was good for us, for Jewish girls, that the German soldiers were afraid of rassenschande. It was said that the rassenschande, so therefore, they-- they were not after us. Well, what was it? Could you define the term?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rassenschande-- it was thought that when Jewish soldiers-- when a German soldier is having something to do with a Jewish woman, then he brings shame for his Aryan race-- race. And therefore, it was said that, therefore, they were afraid of it. And we were left in peace, at least about this. But still, this was very hard physical work, doing all this dragging coal, or heavy things, scrubbing floors, endless row of windows to be cleaned. And practically, shortly, the ghetto changed into a forced labor camp. Because we were being taken in the morning out of the ghetto with-- to different places. And we were coming in the evening-- there. But at least it was still something like a home. At least we still were together with my parents, and so forth. But then, it was another in October. It was another very ruthless thing. And there in in this film, there is shown this courtyard where they throw all the furniture out, and this and that. This was the courtyard where I was during that October deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we were told, first of all, that Jewish policemen were going all around with this list-- they made list-- for people who have to be deported. And this is the reason why people were very much against, actually, Jewish policemen. Because Jewish policemen-- nobody was forced to be a Jewish policeman. They wanted to be. And they even had to maybe pay in order to be a Jewish policeman, because they had privileges. Some were protecting their families. If Germans wanted to deport 500, 1,000 people on the list, they couldn't care less who is on this list. They just wanted 10,000 or 500 Jews. And when the Ordenienst, or the Jewish police, made that list, they, of course, did not put down their families. They protected their families on the account of everybody else. But that other people were not better than their own families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish police subject to the Judenrat or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or governed by it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not governed by Judenrat. They were special unity-- a special unit, yeah. And Judenrat, well, this is where my father didn't want to be-- a part of Judenrat. He just said, even one department of Judenrat, it was the sanitation department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what department were the Jewish police under then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under Jewish police-- Ordenienst,

speaker: interviewer
text: They were under German supervision, essentially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They had German supervision. And therefore, when first during the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, directly under German supervision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Well, there was-- so therefore, first they were coming all over with the list and taking people, and children, and men, and woman, and whatever. But then after-- then after three days, we were told that we have to go on the street, and everybody on the street. And we were standing with-- there, where we work. I was at this time working in that Fliegerhorst. It was a German Luftwaffe base in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were standing there with our friends, one after another, in 16, and the SS were walking around, and shooting and yelling, and, oh, heaven. And then they were making like a selection-- right, left, right, left, right, left, right. And some people were already going, and, to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did not know-- as usually, you never know whether it is better to stay in ghetto, or whether it is better to go outside of ghetto into these big trucks which every day brought us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about two rows ahead of me, the others were led out of the ghetto. And we all were pushed into this courtyard. And this was a very large courtyard. But they were so many people that I was sure that we would be trampled to death before anything, well, happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we were standing there, and a German came suddenly and said, now, everybody of you will go home. First, we did not realize what is going on. But then, everybody was so happy. We are going home. We are not going to be transported somewhere else. But then he says, but in an hour, you have to come back here with your 25 kilo of your things to take. And that-- well, he used the [GERMAN]. It means that the very ancient city of Krakow has to be free of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was what date now? This was-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was October, perhaps 20, 1941. And then, well, she-- they start-- so they said, [GERMAN] of this courtyard. And then people were really trampling one another. And the Germans were shooting to us. And we had absolutely no way of going this way, or that way, or you-- And then, I remember so clearly, I was looking up and then the sun came out. And I thought, if I only get out of this place, I will survive the war. And I really don't know how I get out. But somehow, I found myself on the street. And it was like about a half a block away way, it was that-- that fence, and it was already the street which was free. I mean free, which was for the Aryans. And then, I was-- I really did not know what to do with myself. I did not have energy to go home. I knew that my parents are in my father's office. And I was standing just like this. And then I see that an SS man was trampling in the middle of the street, shooting all over, and standing straight in front of me. And really, there is a limit of human emotions. At this point, I really was beyond fear. I just look at him, just like this. I was a perfect, you know, thing to shoot. But I just look at him and I remember, here I am, like standing and waiting for a streetcar. You can do whatever you want to. And then, since I was absolutely, well, indifferent, I just took at him and then I turn away, like this. And then I hear the shots-- but not to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did he do? I think that suddenly, there was-- unfortunately, there was a mother with children-- two children, one on her arm. And another SS man grabbed the child and psht-- over the wall. And I, till now, I can hear this plask of that head of this little child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this SS man, who was Von Mallotke, who was just in front of me-- he was not this one who grabbed-- he immediately ran and started shooting all over everybody else, but not me. I don't know why. And then another man grabbed me and said, [GERMAN], don't stay here. Your parents are getting crazy there. And I told him, we are supposed to-- go, go. He pulled me out and pushed me into that, well, house door where that was. And then I went to see my parents. But till now, I really don't understand. I think that this terrible beast of man, this Von Mallotke was his name, he was so probably like a dog who runs, runs, runs, runs. And then he noticed that someone is not afraid of him. But I really didn't care at this time, and this was a miracle. So anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Von-- V-O-N. Mallotke-- M-A-L-L-O-T-K-E. He was a famous-- from cruelty, and so forth. So anyway, this is, and then, I-- we stay. Well, our part of the ghetto was already locked. We couldn't come back. After-- at 5 o'clock, the whole SS went out, back home, and the Aussiedlung was, you know, finished. They went like clerks who did their job and went away. And we couldn't go home, so we were staying in that hospital where, after all, the German killed every sick person in this hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- at this time, well, during this. And the same they did in the elders' house. And, of course, the same they did with that orphanage. And, while--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, again, this is October?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is October.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember I couldn't sleep. And I was so hot, I wanted to go and open the window. And I opened the window to the courtyard of this hospital-- it's still there, of course, this house. And I looked down, and I couldn't believe it. They were the dead people, one after another, one after another, one after another. And then I sit down and I wrote a poem about the transportation. About that transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have that poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have this poem and I remember still. But it's in Polish language, and it doesn't make sense to translate. But the end, it was something like this-- well, how the brain of the children on the street, and-- and well, everything, and it ends like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must see it from high above, God. Where are you? Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after this terrible, really terrible Aussiedlung in October, then everybody knew that it's going to be only worse. And people start desperately to run away and trying to find out false papers, and this and that, and that, and that. And then, they start also-- they start also to-- like Schindler, at this time, decide to take his people, his workers to his factory. Any other places did the same. And Fliegerhorst did exactly the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were supposed to be-- all to leave in the Fliegerhorst. And in December 1941, I, with other people who were working at the Fliegerhorst, was taken to this Fliegerhorst. And my parents were still in-- Was that December '41 or December '42?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, you were-- you were in the-- it was from 18th of March, 1941 to December of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 or '42. I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] No, I'm sorry. I think it's '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it would be March 18, '41 through December 1942, and that's when this is taking place now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later on, we were-- completely-- well, I was completely shut off my parents, because I was in the same city, and I couldn't see them. I-- once in a month, I went there, and saw them, but it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's switch tapes now.
